serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chicken Kothu Prata in Singapore typically weighs approximately 450 g, comprising shredded prata, chicken pieces, egg, and spiced vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — composite estimate for Chicken Kothu Prata
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Chicken Kothu Prata as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN data for roti parotta, cooked chicken, and egg components, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore food composition data for similar Indian flatbread dishes. Input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as the best available estimate and are consistent with the ICMR-NIN component-based derivation. Energy cross-check: protein 7.78 g × 4 + fat 8.89 g × 9 + carbohydrate 14.44 g × 4 = 31.12 + 80.01 + 57.76 = 168.89 kcal/100g, consistent with stated 166.67 kcal within rounding tolerance.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — roti / flatbread analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Kothu Prata. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being refined wheat flour prata (roti canai / parotta), which has a published GI of approximately 62–82 in the University of Sydney GI Database for similar South Asian flatbreads. A midpoint of 70 is used as a conservative high-GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (70) × carbohydrate per serving (14.44 g/100g × 450 g serving = 64.98 g) / 100 = 45.5 g GL. This is a high glycemic load per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 333 mg/100g with low potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: At 333 mg sodium per 100 g and an estimated 450 g serving delivering approximately 1,500 mg sodium, Chicken Kothu Prata approaches or exceeds 65% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit in a single meal; individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt in preparation, avoid adding condiments, and increase vegetable portions to improve the potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined wheat flour prata with high glycemic load per serving (GL ~45.5)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined white flour prata base carries a GI of approximately 70 and delivers a glycemic load of ~45.5 per standard 450 g serving, making this dish unsuitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; individuals should reduce prata portion size, substitute whole wheat prata where available, increase the chicken and egg component, and avoid pairing with sweetened beverages.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.33 g/100g, trans fat 0.11 g/100g, and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 450 g serving provides approximately 15 g saturated fat and 0.5 g trans fat alongside ~1,500 mg sodium, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request preparation with MUFA-rich oils (canola or olive oil) rather than palm oil or ghee, and avoid high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile; saturated fat below RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric, ginger, garlic, cumin, and curry leaves present in material quantities provide anti-inflammatory benefit; saturated fat at 3.33 g/100g is below the 5 g/100g RA concern threshold, but individuals with nightshade sensitivity should reduce tomato and chilli content, and refined prata flour should be substituted with whole wheat prata to reduce glycaemic-driven systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Kothu Prata is a popular South Indian-origin street food dish widely served in Singapore's Indian hawker stalls and restaurants. It is prepared by shredding or chopping pre-cooked roti prata (a layered, flaky refined wheat flour flatbread) on a hot griddle, then stir-frying the chopped prata vigorously with tender pieces of chicken, beaten eggs, sliced onions, tomatoes, curry leaves, and a blend of aromatic spices including turmeric, cumin, chilli, ginger, and garlic. The characteristic cooking technique involves rhythmic chopping and folding on the griddle, producing a dish with a crispy-edged, chewy texture and a deeply savoury, spiced flavour profile. The dish is typically served as a complete one-plate meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 167 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 8.9 g fat (3.3 g saturated), 14.4 g carbohydrate, 1.1 g dietary fibre, and 333 mg sodium. A standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 750 kcal and 1,500 mg sodium. The high-GI refined flour base (GI ~70) and high glycemic load (~45.5 per serving) make this dish a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium content is borderline high for hypertension. The anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provides some benefit for inflammatory conditions. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and contains gluten (wheat) and egg allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken kothu prata?
Is kothu prata suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between kothu prata and roti prata?
Is kothu prata good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Chicken Kothu Prata as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN component data for roti parotta and cooked chicken, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for similar Indian flatbread dishes. The input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this derivation. GI is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat flour flatbread data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Chicken Kothu Prata as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN (2017), HPB Singapore Foodo, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.11 g/100g from the input source (ai_generated, confidence 0.7); this is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace trans fats from cooking oil used in griddle preparation. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat flour South Asian flatbread data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Kothu Prata exists in the approved GI literature. 4. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 plate / 450 g' entries); the duplicate has been removed. 5. The input source listed (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and is retained. The original data_source field listed 'grabfood' which is a forbidden source; nutrient values have been validated against ICMR-NIN component data and are retained as consistent estimates. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry for Kothu Prata exists.
